title: Common Genres (With Descriptions, Examples, and Popularity Index)

genres.name: Drama
genres.description: Character-driven stories that emphasize emotional stakes, interpersonal conflict, and consequential choices, often grounded in plausible human experience.

genres.name: Action
genres.description: Stories propelled by physical conflict, high-stakes confrontation, and kinetic set pieces, often centered on survival, rescue, or victory over an adversary.

genres.name: Comedy
genres.description: Stories designed to amuse through humor, incongruity, and social friction, typically resolving tension through reconciliation or restored balance.

genres.name: Thriller
genres.description: Suspense-driven stories focused on danger, uncertainty, and escalating tension, often involving pursuit, conspiracies, or time pressure.
examples: The Silence of the Lambs (1991 film)

genres.name: Fantasy
genres.description: Stories set in worlds with magical rules or supernatural forces, emphasizing wonder, mythic stakes, and transformative journeys.
examples: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring (2001 film)
Harry Potter and the Sorcerer’s Stone (2001 film)

genres.name: Science Fiction
genres.description: Stories exploring speculative technology, future societies, or alternative realities, using 'what if' premises to test ideas and consequences.

genres.name: Adventure
genres.description: Journey-centered stories defined by exploration, discovery, and escalating obstacles, often emphasizing movement through varied settings.
examples: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade (1989 film)
Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl (2003 film)

genres.name: Romance
genres.description: Stories where the central arc focuses on forming, testing, or restoring an intimate relationship, with emotional choice as the primary engine.

genres.name: Romantic Comedy
genres.description: Romance structured around humor and social friction, typically moving from mismatch and misunderstanding toward commitment or reconciliation.

genres.name: Horror
genres.description: Stories designed to evoke fear or dread through threat, the uncanny, or the monstrous, often emphasizing vulnerability and survival.

genres.name: Mystery
genres.description: Investigation-centered stories driven by unanswered questions, clues, and revelations, culminating in explanation or exposure of hidden truth.
examples: The Hound of the Baskervilles (1902 novel)
Murder on the Orient Express (1934 novel)

genres.name: Crime
genres.description: Stories focused on criminal acts and their consequences, often emphasizing investigation, moral compromise, power, and social order.

genres.name: Detective
genres.description: A mystery subgenre centered on a dedicated investigator whose method—logic, observation, or persistence—drives the unraveling of a case.

genres.name: Superhero
genres.description: Stories featuring extraordinary abilities or symbolic vigilante roles, emphasizing identity, moral responsibility, and conflict with major threats.

genres.name: Historical
genres.description: Stories set in a recognizable past, using period detail and historical context as a primary source of stakes and theme.

genres.name: War
genres.description: Stories in which armed conflict is central, emphasizing combat, strategy, survival, and the human cost of violence and duty.
examples: All Quiet on the Western Front (1929 novel)

genres.name: Biography
genres.description: Narratives based on real individuals, emphasizing formative events and character development while balancing fidelity and dramatic structure.
examples: The Wolf of Wall Street (2013 film)

genres.name: Family
genres.description: Stories structured around family relationships and generational dynamics, often blending warmth, conflict, and reconciliation.

genres.name: Young Adult
genres.description: Stories aimed at teen readers, typically centering identity formation, social belonging, first love, and boundary-testing choices.
examples: The Fault in Our Stars (2012 novel)
The Perks of Being a Wallflower (1999 novel)

genres.name: Literary Fiction
genres.description: Character- and language-forward stories that prioritize psychological depth, thematic complexity, and stylistic craft over plot mechanics.

genres.name: Children’s Literature
genres.description: Stories written primarily for children, emphasizing clarity, imaginative engagement, and moral or emotional learning at age-appropriate stakes.
examples: The Cat in the Hat (1957 book)

genres.name: Satire
genres.description: Stories that use humor, exaggeration, or irony to critique social norms, institutions, or hypocrisy.

genres.name: Psychological
genres.description: Stories emphasizing internal conflict, perception, and mental pressure, often exploring obsession, identity fracture, or unreliable reality.

genres.name: Western
genres.description: Stories set in frontier contexts, emphasizing lawlessness, moral codes, rugged individualism, and clashes over land and order.
examples: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (1966 film)

genres.name: Political
genres.description: Stories driven by governance, power struggles, ideology, and institutional pressure, often emphasizing strategy and moral tradeoffs.

genres.name: Musical
genres.description: Stories in which songs and performance are central narrative devices, advancing plot, character, and emotional turning points.

genres.name: Holiday
genres.description: Seasonal stories anchored to a holiday context, emphasizing tradition, reunion, redemption, and heightened emotional warmth or contrast.
